description: Figure 1 comprises two sub-panels quantifying the effects of alloying additions on the physical properties of ferritic iron. Panel (a) plots the density reduction as a function of alloy content (0–16 wt%), showing that carbon and aluminum produce the steepest decreases (density drops from ~7.87 g/cm³ to ~7.0 g/cm³ for 2 wt% C and to ~6.1 g/cm³ for 16 wt% Al), whereas chromium and manganese exhibit gentle negative slopes. Panel (b) illustrates the degradation of Young’s modulus in annealed Fe-Al steels with increasing aluminum content (0–16 wt%), compiling experimental data from Frommeyer [24], Kubo [28], Yamamoto-Taniguchi [28], and Fukuroi-Shibuya [28]. The modulus declines from ~215 GPa to approximately 120–160 GPa, with a quantified reduction rate of 2–2.5% per 1 wt% Al attributed to decreased lattice energy and increased atomic spacing. Together, these charts establish the fundamental compromise in Fe-Mn-Al-C low-density steels: aluminum addition reduces density favorably for lightweight automotive applications but simultaneously lowers the elastic modulus unfavorably, limiting specific stiffness performance.
